comment: Records all chat or inline Assistant interactions in order to see engagement and adoption.

columns.comment: ID of the account.
ID of the workspace.
A unique ID for this event.
Time that the event happened. Timezone information is recorded at the end of the value with +00:00 representing UTC.
Date that the event happened.
Origination of request.
Email of the user initiating the request.

comment: The audit logs table records detailed information about actions and events occurring within a Databricks workspace or account. It is used for security auditing, compliance, and monitoring user and system activity. Each row represents a single event, such as a user login, data access, permission change, or system operation. See documentation of services and actions at https://docs.databricks.com/aws/admin/account-settings/audit-logs

columns.comment: The Databricks account identifier associated with the audit event.
The Databricks workspace in which the audit event took place. For account-level events, the workspace_id will be 0.
Audit log schema version
Timestamp of the event
The calendar date when the event or action occurred. Useful for filtering and aggregating events by day.
The IP address from which the request originated.
The user agent string or identifier describing the client, browser, or tool that initiated the request (e.g., web browser, API client, CLI)
Unique identifier for the session in which the request was made. Sessions group related actions by a user or service over a period of time

A map of key/value pairs that can be used to identify the person or service principal that initiated the request. Contains keys such as email and subject_name. An email of System-User is used for background system tasks.
The name of the Databricks service that generated the audit event. See [service documentation](https://docs.databricks.com/aws/admin/account-settings/audit-logs#audit-log-services) for details.

The name of the action that has been performed as part of the audit event. Action names vary depending on the Databricks service (service_name). See [documentation of actions per service](https://docs.databricks.com/aws/admin/account-settings/audit-logs) for details.
A unique identifier of the request.

A map of key/value pairs with the request parameters. Request parameters vary by request type. See [documentation of request parameters per action](https://docs.databricks.com/aws/admin/account-settings/audit-logs) for details.
Struct of response, includes status_code, error_messages, and where applicable a result string. status_code is an HTTP status code.
Indicates whether the audit event is at the workspace or account level. Either `ACCOUNT_LEVEL` or `WORKSPACE_LEVEL`.
A unique identifier of the audit event.

A struct with the identities from the audit event. This includes `run_by` (the identity who initiated the event) and `run_as` (the identity being used for authorisation purposes). See [Auditing group dedicated compute activity documentation](https://docs.databricks.com/aws/compute/group-access#auditing-group-dedicated-compute-activity) for more details.

comment: The clean_room_events table records actions taken by you or your collaborators on clean rooms (Public Preview) in your account. This table includes regional data from across your account.

columns.comment: The ID of the Databricks account.
The ID of the Unity Catalog metastore.
The ID of the clean room event
Name of the clean room associated with the event
The ID of central clean room
Global metastore ID of the collaborator who initiated the event
The type of the event. See [Logged clean room events](https://docs.databricks.com/en/admin/system-tables/clean-rooms.html#events).
The metadata of the event type CLEAN_ROOM_CREATED
The metadata of the event type CLEAN_ROOM_DELETED
The metadata of the event type RUN_NOTEBOOK_STARTED
The metadata of the event type RUN_NOTEBOOK_COMPLETED
The metadata of the event type CLEAN_ROOM_ASSETS_UPDATED
Timestamp when the event took place
The metadata of the event type OUTPUT_SCHEMA_DELETED
The alias of the collaborator who initiated the event
The metadata of the event type ASSET_REVIEW_CREATED

comment: The column lineage system table includes a record for each write event (Spark operation) on a Unity Catalog table or path. This includes but is not limited to job, notebook, and dashboard runs. The column lineage table does not include events that do not have a source (e.g. inserting explicit values). Please refer to the [documentation](https://docs.databricks.com/en/data-governance/unity-catalog/data-lineage.html#limitations) for limitations.

columns.comment: The id of the Databricks account.
The id of the Unity Catalog metastore.
The id of the workspace
The type of entity the lineage transaction was captured from. The supported value is NOTEBOOK, JOB, PIPELINE, DASHBOARD_V3 (AI/BI Dashboard), DBSQL_DASHBOARD (Legacy dashboard), DBSQL_QUERY, OR NULL.
The id of the entity the lineage transaction was captured from. If entity_type is NULL, entity_id is NULL.

id to describe the unique run of the entity, or NULL. This differs for each entity type:

Notebook: command_run_id

Job: job_run_id

Databricks SQL query: statement_id

Dashboard: statement_id

Legacy dashboard: statement_id

Pipeline: pipeline_update_id

If entity_type is NULL, entity_run_id is NULL. Records with statement_id and job_run_id can be joined with the query history and jobs system tables respectively.
Three-level name to identify the source table.
The catalog of the source table.
The schema of the source table.
The name of the source table.
Location in cloud storage of the source table, or the path if it’s reading from cloud storage directly.
The type of the source. The value is TABLE, PATH, VIEW, MATERIALIZED_VIEW, METRIC_VIEW, or STREAMING_TABLE.
The name of the source column.
Three-level name to identify the target table.
The catalog of the target table.
The schema of the target table.
The name of the target table.
Location in cloud storage of the target table.
The type of the target. The value is TABLE, PATH, VIEW, MATERIALIZED_VIEW, METRIC_VIEW, or STREAMING_TABLE.
The name of the target column.
The user who generated this lineage. This can be a Databricks username, a Databricks service principal ID, “System-User”, or NULL if the user information cannot be captured.
The timestamp when the lineage was generated. Timezone information is recorded at the end of the value with +00:00 representing UTC.
The date when the lineage was generated. This is a partitioned column.
Primary key of each row, it is auto-generated and cannot be joined with any tables

One query or one spark job run could append multiple lineage rows, this event_id is a unique id to group the rows that belong to the same event. This is generated in the pipeline and cannot be joined with any tables.
A foreign key to join with query history system table. It is set when a query is from a warehouse or serverless warehouse.
It is a list of ids of the query context which is joinable with other system tables.

comment: The inbound network access control table records policy evaluation events for incoming network requests. Each row represents a single access control policy evaluation.

The network access events table records events where internet access is denied. Each row represents an individual event. For example, if a user attempts to access “google.com” from a notebook and fails, the failure would be logged as an event.

columns.comment: The ID of the Databricks account
The ID of the workspace where the event occurred
The type of destination. Possible values are DNS, IP, and STORAGE
Details of the blocked destination. Depending on the destination type, the value could be a domain name, IP address, or storage location.
Details about the DNS destination. Only populates for DNS destinations, otherwise the field is NULL.
Details about the storage destination. Only populates for storage destinations, otherwise the field is NULL.
Timestamp when the event took place
Type of access event that occurred.
The ID of the event
The source type of the network event, mainly for customer debugging

comment: The table lineage system table includes a record for each read or write event (Spark operation) on a UC table or path. This includes but is not limited to job, notebook, and dashboard runs. This table can be joined with other system tables such as Query History and Jobs to get additional information about the read/write operation. Please refer to the [documentation](https://docs.databricks.com/en/data-governance/unity-catalog/data-lineage.html#limitations) for limitations.

relationships.core_columns: system.access.table_lineage.job_id = system.billing.usage.job_id AND system.access.table_lineage.job_run_id = system.billing.usage.job_run_id AND system.access.table_lineage.workspace_id = system.billing.usage.workspace_id

columns.comment: The id of the Databricks account.
The id of the Unity Catalog metastore.
The id of the workspace
The type of entity the lineage transaction was captured from. The value is NOTEBOOK, JOB, PIPELINE, DASHBOARD_V3 (Dashboard), DBSQL_DASHBOARD (Legacy dashboard), DBSQL_QUERY, OR NULL.
The ID of the entity the lineage transaction was captured from. If entity_type is NULL, entity_id is NULL.

id to describe the unique run of the entity, or NULL. This differs for each entity type:

Notebook: command_run_id

Job: job_run_id

Databricks SQL query: statement_id

Dashboard: statement_id

Legacy dashboard: statement_id

Pipeline: pipeline_update_id

If entity_type is NULL, entity_run_id is NULL. Records with statement_id and job_run_id can be joined with the query history and jobs system tables respectively.
Three-part name to identify the source table.
The catalog of the source table.
The schema of the source table.
The name of the source table.
Location in cloud storage of the source table, or the path if it’s reading from cloud storage directly.
The type of the source. The value is TABLE, PATH, VIEW, MATERIALIZED_VIEW, METRIC_VIEW, or STREAMING_TABLE.
Three-part name to identify the target table.
The catalog of the target table.
The schema of the target table.
The name of the target table.
Location in cloud storage of the target table.
The type of the target. The value is TABLE, PATH, VIEW, MATERIALIZED_VIEW, METRIC_VIEW, or STREAMING_TABLE.
The user who generated this lineage. This can be a Databricks username, a Databricks service principal ID, “System-User”, or NULL if the user information cannot be captured.
The timestamp when the lineage was generated. Timezone information is recorded at the end of the value with +00:00 representing UTC.
The date when the lineage was generated. This is a partitioned column.
Primary key of each row, it is auto-generated and cannot be joined with any tables

One query or one spark job run could append multiple lineage rows, this event_id is a unique id to group the rows that belong to the same event. This is generated in the pipeline and cannot be joined with any tables.
A foreign key to join with query history system table. It is set when a query is from a warehouse or serverless warehouse.
It is a list of ids of the query context which is joinable with other system tables.

comment: The workspaces system table is a slow-changing dimension table of metadata for all the workspaces in your account.

columns.comment: The ID of the parent account of this workspace.
The ID of the workspace.
The human-readable name of the workspace.
URL of the databricks workspace.
Timestamp of when the workspace was created.
Lifecycle status of the workspace: NOT_PROVISIONED | PROVISIONING | RUNNING | FAILED | BANNED
ID of the account this report was generated for
The time this price became effective in UTC
The time this price stopped being effective in UTC
Name of the SKU
Name of the Cloud this price is applicable to. Possible values are AWS, AZURE, and GCP.
The currency this price is expressed in
The unit of measurement that is monetized.

comment: The [pricing table](https://docs.databricks.com/admin/system-tables/pricing.html) gives you access to a historical log of SKU pricing. A record gets added each time there is a change to a SKU price.

columns.comment: ID of the account this report was generated for
The time this price became effective in UTC
The time this price stopped being effective in UTC
Name of the SKU
Name of the Cloud this price is applicable to. Possible values are AWS, AZURE, and GCP.
The currency this price is expressed in
The unit of measurement that is monetized.

A structured data field that includes pricing info at the published list price rate. The key default will always return a single price that can be used for simple long-term estimates. The key promotional represents a temporary promotional price that all customers get which could be used for cost estimation during the temporary period. The key effective_list resolves list and promotional price, and contains the effective list price used for calculating the cost.

comment: The [usage table](https://docs.databricks.com/admin/system-tables/billing.html) gives you access to account-wide billable usage data. The data is centralized and routed to all the regions.

extended_conditions.condition: system.billing.list_prices.cloud = system.billing.usage.cloud AND system.billing.list_prices.sku_name = system.billing.usage.sku_name AND system.billing.list_prices.usage_unit = system.billing.usage.usage_unit

system.billing.list_prices.account_id = system.billing.usage.account_id AND system.billing.list_prices.cloud = system.billing.usage.cloud AND system.billing.list_prices.sku_name = system.billing.usage.sku_name AND system.billing.list_prices.usage_unit = system.billing.usage.usage_unit

system.billing.list_prices.account_id = system.billing.usage.account_id AND system.billing.list_prices.sku_name = system.billing.usage.sku_name AND system.billing.list_prices.usage_unit = system.billing.usage.usage_unit

system.billing.list_prices.account_id = system.billing.usage.account_id AND system.billing.list_prices.cloud = system.billing.usage.cloud AND system.billing.list_prices.sku_name = system.billing.usage.sku_name

system.billing.list_prices.account_id = system.billing.usage.account_id AND system.billing.list_prices.price_start_time = system.billing.usage.usage_time AND system.billing.list_prices.sku_name = system.billing.usage.sku_name

system.billing.account_prices.account_id = system.billing.usage.account_id AND system.billing.account_prices.cloud = system.billing.usage.cloud AND system.billing.account_prices.usage_unit = system.billing.usage.usage_unit

system.billing.usage.change_time = system.compute.clusters.change_time AND system.billing.usage.cluster_id = system.compute.clusters.cluster_id AND system.billing.usage.workspace_id = system.compute.clusters.workspace_id

system.access.audit.account_id = system.billing.usage.account_id AND system.access.audit.event_date = system.billing.usage.usage_time AND system.access.audit.workspace_id = system.billing.usage.workspace_id

relationships.core_columns: system.billing.cloud_infra_cost.account_id = system.billing.usage.account_id AND system.billing.cloud_infra_cost.record_id = system.billing.usage.record_id AND system.billing.cloud_infra_cost.usage_time = system.billing.usage.usage_time

columns.comment: ID of the account this report was generated for
ID of the Workspace this usage was associated with
Unique ID for this usage record
Name of the SKU
Cloud this usage is relevant for. Possible values are AWS, AZURE, and GCP.
The start time relevant to this usage record. Timezone information is recorded at the end of the value with +00:00 representing UTC timezone.
The end time relevant to this usage record. Timezone information is recorded at the end of the value with +00:00 representing UTC timezone.
Date of the usage record, this field can be used for faster aggregation by date
Tags applied by the users to this usage. Includes compute resource tags and jobs tags.
Unit this usage is measured in. Possible values include DBUs.
Number of units consumed for this record.

System-provided metadata about the usage, including IDs for compute resources and jobs (if applicable). See [Analyze usage metadata](https://docs.databricks.com/en/admin/system-tables/billing.html#usage-metadata).
System-provided metadata about the identities involved in the usage. See [Analyze identity metadata](https://docs.databricks.com/en/admin/system-tables/billing.html#identity-metadata).

Whether the record is original, a retraction, or a restatement. The value is ORIGINAL unless the record is related to a correction. See [Analyze correction records](https://docs.databricks.com/en/admin/system-tables/billing.html#record-type).
Date the record was ingested into the usage table.

The product that originated the usage. Some products can be billed as different SKUs. For possible values, see [View information about the product associated with the usage](https://docs.databricks.com/en/admin/system-tables/billing.html#features).
Details about the specific product features used.
The type of usage attributed to the product or workload for billing purposes. Possible values are COMPUTE_TIME, STORAGE_SPACE, NETWORK_BYTES, API_CALLS, TOKEN, or GPU_TIME.

comment: The cluster table is a slow-changing dimension table that contains the full history of compute configurations over time for all-purpose and jobs compute.

columns.comment: ID of the account where this cluster was created.
ID of the workspace where this cluster was created.
ID of the cluster for which this record is associated.
User defined name for the cluster.
example_filters: Field Eng Shared UC LTS Cluster

columns.comment: Username of the cluster owner. Defaults to the cluster creator, but can be changed through the [Clusters API](https://docs.databricks.com/api/workspace/clusters/changeowner).
Timestamp of the change to this compute definition.
Timestamp of when the cluster was deleted. The value is null if the cluster is not deleted.
Driver node type name. This matches the instance type name from the cloud provider.
Worker node type name. This matches the instance type name from the cloud provider.
Number of workers. Defined for fixed-size clusters only.
The set minimum number of workers. This field is valid only for autoscaling clusters.
The set maximum number of workers. This field is valid only for autoscaling clusters.
The configured autotermination duration.
Autoscaling disk enablement status.
User-defined tags for the cluster (does not include default tags).
Where the cluster was created. For all-purpose compute, the source could be `UI` or `API`. Other compute types are logged as `JOB`, `PIPELINE`, or `PIPELINE_MAINTENANCE`.
Set of paths for init scripts.
AWS specific settings.
Azure specific settings.
GCP specific settings.
Instance pool ID if the driver is configured on top of an instance pool.
Instance Pool ID if the worker is configured on top of an instance pool.
The Databricks Runtime of the cluster.
Timestamp of change to the compute definition.
Change date. Used for retention.
Data security mode decides what data governance model to use when accessing data from a cluster.
The ID of the cluster policy used to create the cluster if applicable.

comment: The node timeline table captures node-level resource utilization data at minute granularity. Each record contains data for a given minute of time per instance.

columns.comment: ID of the account where this compute resource is running.
ID of the workspace where this compute resource is running.
ID of the compute resource.
ID for the specific instance.
Start time for the record in UTC.
End time for the record in UTC.
Whether the instance is a driver or worker node.
Percentage of time the CPU spent in userland.
Percentage of time the CPU spent in the kernel.
Percentage of time the CPU spent waiting for I/O.
Percentage of the compute’s memory that was used during the time period (including memory used by background processes running on the compute).
Percentage of memory usage attributed to memory swap.
The number of bytes sent out in network traffic.
The number of received bytes from network traffic.
The disk utilization grouped by mount point. This is ephemeral storage provisioned only while the compute is running.
The name of the node type. This will match the instance type name from the cloud provider.
Private IP.

comment: The node type table captures the currently available node types with their basic hardware information

columns.comment: ID of the account where this node type is available.
Unique identifier for node type.
Number of vCPUs for the instance.
Total memory for the instance.
Number of GPUs for the instance.

comment: The Warehouse events table table captures events related to SQL warehouses. For example, starting, stopping, running, scaling up and down

columns.comment: The ID of the Databricks account.
The ID of the workspace where the warehouse is deployed.
The ID of SQL warehouse the event is related to.
The type of warehouse event. Possible values are SCALED_UP, SCALED_DOWN, STOPPING, RUNNING, STARTING, and STOPPED.
The number of clusters that are actively running.
Timestamp of when the event took place in UTC.

comment: The Warehouses table contains the full history of configurations over time for any SQL warehouse.

columns.comment: The ID of the SQL warehouse.
The ID of the workspace where the warehouse is deployed.
The ID of the Databricks account.
The name of the SQL warehouse.
The type of SQL warehouse. Possible values are CLASSIC, PRO, and SERVERLESS.
The channel of the SQL warehouse. Possible values are CURRENT and PREVIEW.
The cluster size of the SQL warehouse. Possible values are 2X_SMALL, X_SMALL, SMALL, MEDIUM, LARGE, X_LARGE, 2X_LARGE, 3X_LARGE, 4X_LARGE, and 5X_LARGE.
The minimum number of clusters permitted.
The maximum number of clusters permitted.
The number of minutes before the SQL warehouse auto-stops due to inactivity.
Tags for the SQL warehouse.
Timestamp of change to the SQL warehouse definition.
Timestamp of when the SQL warehouse was deleted. The value is null if the SQL warehouse is not deleted.
The name of the Databricks principal or user that created this warehouse.

comment: The `job_run_timeline` table is a fact table that contains slices of ongoing job runs, allowing one to track the run time and metadata related to each job run. Each slice is represented by `period_start_time` and `period_end_time` timestamps. For documentation, example queries, and monitoring usage, see [Lakeflow System Tables](https://docs.databricks.com/en/admin/system-tables/jobs.html#job-run-timeline-table-schema).

extended_conditions.condition: system.billing.usage.job_id = system.lakeflow.job_run_timeline.job_id AND system.billing.usage.job_run_id = system.lakeflow.job_run_timeline.run_id AND system.billing.usage.workspace_id = system.lakeflow.job_run_timeline.workspace_id

system.billing.usage.job_id = system.lakeflow.job_run_timeline.job_id AND system.billing.usage.run_id = system.lakeflow.job_run_timeline.run_id AND system.billing.usage.workspace_id = system.lakeflow.job_run_timeline.workspace_id

columns.comment: The ID of the account this job belongs to.
The ID of the workspace this job belongs to.
The ID of the job. This key is only unique within a single workspace.
The ID of the job run.
The start time for the run or for the time period. Timezone information is recorded at the end of the value with +00:00 representing UTC.
The end time for the run or for the time period. Timezone information is recorded at the end of the value with +00:00 representing UTC.
The type of trigger that can fire a run. For possible values, see [Trigger type values](https://docs.databricks.com/en/admin/system-tables/jobs.html#trigger)
The outcome of the job run. For possible values, see [Result state values](https://docs.databricks.com/en/admin/system-tables/jobs.html#result).
The type of job run. For possible values, see [Run type values](https://docs.databricks.com/en/admin/system-tables/jobs.html#run-type).
The user-supplied run name associated with this job run.

Array containing the compute IDs for the parent job run. Use for identifying cluster used by `WORKFLOW_RUN` run types. For other compute information, refer to the `job_task_run_timeline` table. **Not populated for rows emitted before late August 2024**

The termination code of the job run. For possible values, see [Termination code values](https://docs.databricks.com/en/admin/system-tables/jobs.html#termination). **Not populated for rows emitted before late August 2024.**
The job-level parameters used in the job run. **Not populated for rows emitted before late August 2024.**
The id of the source task run. Use for identifying which task run triggered this job run. **Not populated for rows emitted before late November 2025**
The id of the root task run. Use for identifying which task run triggered this job run. **Not populated for rows emitted before late November 2025**
Details about the compute resources used in the job run. **Not populated for rows emitted before late November 2025**
The type of termination for the job run. **Not populated for rows emitted before late November 2025**
The duration of the setup phase for the job run in seconds. **Not populated for rows emitted before late November 2025**
The duration spent in the queue for the job run in seconds. **Not populated for rows emitted before late November 2025**
The total duration of the job run in seconds. **Not populated for rows emitted before late November 2025**
The duration of the cleanup phase for the job run in seconds. **Not populated for rows emitted before late November 2025**
The duration of the execution phase for the job run in seconds. **Not populated for rows emitted before late November 2025**

comment: The `job_task_run_timeline` table is a fact table that contains slices of ongoing job runs, allowing one to track the run time and metadata related to each job task run. Each slice is represented by `period_start_time` and `period_end_time` timestamps. For documentation, example queries, and monitoring usage, see [Lakeflow System Tables](https://docs.databricks.com/en/admin/system-tables/jobs.html#job-task-run-timeline-table-schema).

columns.comment: The ID of the account this job belongs to.
The ID of the workspace this job belongs to.
The ID of the job. Only unique within a single workspace.
The ID of the task run.
The start time for the task or for the time period. Timezone information is recorded at the end of the value with +00:00 representing UTC.
The end time for the task or for the time period. Timezone information is recorded at the end of the value with +00:00 representing UTC.
The reference key for a task in a job. This key is only unique within a single job.
An array containing the IDs of compute used by the job task.
The outcome of the job task run.
The ID of the job run. **Not populated for rows emitted before late August 2024.**
The ID of the parent run. **Not populated for rows emitted before late August 2024.**
The termination code of the task run. **Not populated for rows emitted before late August 2024.**
Details about the compute resources used in the job task run. **Not populated for rows emitted before late November 2025**
The type of termination for the job task run. **Not populated for rows emitted before late November 2025**
The task-level parameters used in the job task run. **Not populated for rows emitted before late November 2025**
The duration of the setup phase for the task run in seconds. **Not populated for rows emitted before late November 2025**
The duration of the cleanup phase for the task run in seconds. **Not populated for rows emitted before late November 2025**
The duration of the execution phase for the task run in seconds. **Not populated for rows emitted before late November 2025**

comment: The `job_tasks` table is a slow-changing dimension table (SCD2) that contains the full history of job task configurations over time. When a row changes, a new row is emitted, logically replacing the previous one. For documentation, example queries, and monitoring usage, see [Lakeflow System Tables](https://docs.databricks.com/en/admin/system-tables/jobs.html#job-task-table-schema).

columns.comment: The ID of the account this job belongs to.
The ID of the workspace this job belongs to.
The ID of the job. Only unique within a single workspace.
The reference key for a task in a job. Only unique within a single job.
The task keys of all upstream dependencies of this task.
The time when the task was last modified. Timezone recorded as +00:00 (UTC).
The time when a task was deleted by the user. Timezone recorded as +00:00 (UTC).
The timeout duration for the task in seconds. **Not populated for rows emitted before late November 2025**
Set of health rules defined for this job task. **Not populated for rows emitted before late November 2025**

comment: The `jobs` table is a slow-changing dimension table (SCD2) that contains the full history of job configurations over time. When a row changes, a new row is emitted, logically replacing the previous one. For documentation, example queries, and monitoring usage, see [Lakeflow System Tables](https://docs.databricks.com/en/admin/system-tables/jobs.html#jobs-table-schema).

columns.comment: The ID of the account this job belongs to.
The ID of the workspace this job belongs to.
The ID of the job. Only unique within a single workspace.
The user-supplied name of the job.
example_filters: [dev prashanth_subrahmanyam] [dev] Health Monitor - Gold Layer Data Pipeline

columns.comment: The ID of the principal who created the job.
The user-supplied custom tags associated with this job.
The ID of the user or service principal whose permissions are used for the job run.
The time when the job was last modified. Timezone recorded as +00:00 (UTC).
The time when the job was deleted by the user. Timezone recorded as +00:00 (UTC).
The user-supplied description of the job. **Not populated for rows emitted before late August 2024.**
The trigger configuration for the job. **Not populated for rows emitted before late November 2025**
The type of trigger for the job. **Not populated for rows emitted before late November 2025**
The email/ID of the service principal whose permissions are used for the job run. **Not populated for rows emitted before late November 2025**
The email/ID of the service principal who created the job. **Not populated for rows emitted before late November 2025**
Indicates whether the job is paused. **Not populated for rows emitted before late November 2025**
The timeout duration for the job in seconds. **Not populated for rows emitted before late November 2025**
Set of health rules defined for this job. **Not populated for rows emitted before late November 2025**
Deployment information for jobs managed by external sources. **Not populated for rows emitted before late November 2025**
The time at which this job was created. Timezone recorded as +00:00 (UTC). **Not populated for rows emitted before late November 2025**

comment: The `pipeline_update_timeline` table is a fact table that contains slices of ongoing pipeline updates, allowing one to track the execution time and metadata related to each pipeline update. Each slice is represented by `period_start_time` and `period_end_time` timestamps.

columns.comment: The ID of the workspace this pipeline belongs to.
The ID of the pipeline. Unique within a workspace.
The ID of the pipeline update.
The type of the pipeline update.
The ID of the request. Helps to understand how many times an update had to be retried/restarted.
The email/ID of the service principal whose permissions are used for the pipeline update.
What triggered this update.
The details of the pipeline's trigger.
The outcome of the pipeline update.
Details about the compute resource used in the pipeline update.
The start time for the pipeline update or for the hour. The value is stored as a UTC timestamp.
The end time for the pipeline update or for the hour. The value is stored as a UTC timestamp.
A list of tables to update without fullRefresh.
A list of tables to update with fullRefresh.
A list of streaming flows to clear the checkpoints for.
The ID of the account this pipeline belongs to.

comment: The `pipelines` table is a slow-changing dimension table (SCD2) that contains the full history of pipeline configurations over time. When a row changes, a new row is emitted, logically replacing the previous one.

columns.comment: The ID of the workspace this pipeline belongs to.
The ID of the pipeline.
The type of pipeline.
The user-supplied name of the pipeline.
The email/ID of the service principal who created the pipeline.
The email/ID of the service principal whose permissions are used for the pipeline run.
The user-supplied tags associated with the pipeline.
The settings of the pipeline.
The user-supplied configuration of the pipeline.
The time when the pipeline was last modified. Timezone recorded as +00:00 (UTC).
The time when a pipeline was deleted by the user. Timezone recorded as +00:00 (UTC).
The ID of the account this pipeline belongs to.
The time when a pipeline was created by the user. Timezone recorded as +00:00 (UTC). **Not populated for rows emitted before late November 2025**

comment: A table that stores all data related to records ingested using Zerobus Ingest
A table that stores all data related to stream events incurred by Zerobus Ingest usage

columns.comment: ID of the created stream
Time of log event
ID of the workspace that the stream is created in
ID of the account that owns the given workspace
Custom ID of the client producer application used to ingest data
Time of opening the stream
Time of closing the stream
ID of the table that is being written into
Fully qualified table name in human readable format
Protocol used for data ingestion
Data format used for passing data
The errors that occurred while using the stream

comment: Each row represents an experiment created in the databricks managed MLflow system. An experiment represents a user defined collection of model training runs

columns.comment: The ID of the account containing the MLflow experiment
The system time when the experiment was last updated
The system time when the MLflow experiment was soft-deleted by the user
The ID of the workspace containing the MLflow experiment
The ID of the MLflow experiment
User-provided name of the experiment
The system time when the experiment was created

comment: Holds the timeseries metrics logged to MLflow associated with a given model training, evaluation, or agent development. Each row represents one datapoint in the metric timeseries.

columns.comment: The ID of the account containing the MLflow run to which the metric was logged
The system time when the metric was inserted
A unique identifier of the metric to distinguish between identical values
The ID of the workspace containing the MLflow run to which the metric was logged
The ID of the MLflow experiment containing the MLflow run to which the metric was logged
The ID of the MLflow run to which the metric was logged
The metric name
The user-specified time when the metric was computed
The step (e.g. epoch) of model training or agent development at which the metric was logged
The metric value

comment: Each row represents a run created in the databricks managed MLflow system. A run holds metadata associated with a model training, agent development, or evaluation. This table holds the summary information on each run, not the entire history. For metrics, you can consult run_metrics_history to see the complete logged time series.

columns.comment: The ID of the account containing the MLflow run
The system time when the run was last updated
The system time when the MLflow run was soft-deleted by the user
The ID of the workspace containing the MLflow run
The ID of the MLflow run
The ID of the MLflow experiment containing the MLflow run
The name of the Databricks principal or user that created the MLflow run
The user-specified time when the MLflow run started
The user-specified time when the MLflow run ended
The name of the MLflow run
The execution status of the MLflow run
Key-value parameters of the MLflow run
Key-value tags set on the MLflow run
An aggregated view summarizing the metrics in the run_metrics_history

comment: The query history table, located at system.query.history, includes records for every SQL statement run using SQL warehouses. The table includes account-wide records from all workspaces in the same region from which you access the table.

extended_conditions.condition: system.billing.usage.event_date_jst = system.query.history.event_date_jst AND system.billing.usage.hhmm_jst = system.query.history.hhmm_jst AND system.billing.usage.warehouse_id = system.query.history.warehouse_id AND system.billing.usage.workspace_id = system.query.history.workspace_id

columns.comment: ID of the account.
The ID of the workspace where the query was run.
The ID that uniquely identifies the execution of the statement. You can use this ID to find the statement execution in the Query History UI.
The email address or username of the user who ran the statement.
The Spark session ID.

The statement termination state. Possible values are:

FINISHED: execution was successful

FAILED: execution failed with the reason for failure described in the accompanying error message

CANCELED: execution was canceled
A struct that represents the type of compute resource used to run the statement and the ID of the resource where applicable. The type value will be WAREHOUSE.
The ID of the user who ran the statement.
Text of the SQL statement. If you have configured customer-managed keys, statement_text is empty.
The statement type. For example: ALTER, COPY, and`INSERT`.
Message describing the error condition. If you have configured customer-managed keys, error_message is empty.
Client application that ran the statement. For example: Databricks SQL, Tableau, and Power BI.
The connector used to connect to Databricks to run the statement. For example: Databricks SQL Driver for Go, Databricks ODBC Driver, Databricks JDBC Driver.
Total execution time of the statement in milliseconds ( excluding result fetch time ).
Time spent waiting for compute resources to be provisioned in milliseconds.
Time spent waiting in queue for available compute capacity in milliseconds.
Time spent executing the statement in milliseconds.
Time spent loading metadata and optimizing the statement in milliseconds.

The sum of all task durations in milliseconds. This time represents the combined time it took to run the query across all cores of all nodes. It can be significantly longer than the wall-clock duration if multiple tasks are executed in parallel. It can be shorter than the wall-clock duration if tasks wait for available nodes.
Time spent, in milliseconds, fetching the statement results after the execution finished.
The time when Databricks received the request. Timezone information is recorded at the end of the value with +00:00 representing UTC.
The time the statement execution ended, excluding result fetch time. Timezone information is recorded at the end of the value with +00:00 representing UTC.
The time the statement last received a progress update. Timezone information is recorded at the end of the value with +00:00 representing UTC.
The number of table partitions read after table partition pruning.
The number of pruned files during table partition and file pruning.
The number of files read after table partition and file pruning.
The number of rows output from scans after table partition, file, in file and row pruning.
Total number of rows returned by the statement.
The number of bytes of files read after IO pruning.
The percentage of bytes of persistent data read from the IO cache.
TRUE indicates that the statement result was fetched from the cache.
Size of data, in bytes, temporarily written to disk while executing the statement.
The size in bytes of persistent data written to cloud object storage.
The total amount of data in bytes sent over the network.

A struct that contains key-value pairs representing one or more Databricks entities that were involved in the execution of this statement, such as jobs, notebooks, or dashboards. This field only records Databricks entities and are not sorted by execution order. Statement executions that contain multiple IDs indicate that the execution was triggered by multiple entities: for example, an Alert may trigger on a Job result and call a SQL Query, so all three IDs will be populated within query_source.
The ID of the user or service principal whose privilege was used to run the statement.
The name of the user or service principal whose privilege was used to run the statement.
Total number of rows of persistent data written to cloud object storage.
The number of files of persistent data written to cloud object storage.
Statement id of query that inserted result in cache when result is fetched from cache, otherwise store the query's statement id.
Query parameters values associated with the query. Only one of named_parameters or pos_parameters can be populated.
The user-supplied custom tags associated with this statement execution.
The number of bytes of files pruned after table partition and file pruning.
The number of bytes of files read after table partition and file pruning.

comment: This system table records per request usage information for model serving. For instance, it can be used to attribute how many LLM tokens were sent to different served entities. To look up more information about the served entity that received the request, use the served_entity_id as the join key with system.serving.served_entities.

columns.comment: The workspace ID for the workspace in which the serving endpoint exists.
The Databricks account ID of the workspace associated with the serving endpoint.
The user provided request identifier that can be specified in the model serving request body.
A Databricks generated request identifier attached to all model serving requests.
The ID of the user or service principal whose permissions are used for the invocation request of the serving endpoint.
The HTTP status code that was returned from the model.
The timestamp at which the request is received.
The token count of the input.
The token count of the output.
The character count of the input string or prompt.
The character count of the output string of the response.

The user provided map containing identifiers of the end user or the customer application that makes the call to the endpoint. See [Further define usage with usage_context](https://docs.databricks.com/en/ai-gateway/configure-ai-gateway-endpoints.html#usage-context).
Whether the request is in stream mode.
The unique ID used to join with the system.serving.served_entities dimension table to lookup information about the endpoint and served entity.

comment: This system table records all entities that were served through Databricks model serving as well as the configurations at different config versions.

columns.comment: The unique ID of the served entity.
The Databricks account ID of the workspace associated with the serving endpoint.
The workspace ID for the workspace in which the serving endpoint exists.
The ID of the creator.
The name of the serving endpoint.
The unique ID of the serving endpoint.
The name of the served entity.
Type of the entity that is served. Can be FEATURE_SPEC, EXTERNAL_MODEL, FOUNDATION_MODEL, or CUSTOM_MODEL
The underlying name of the entity. Different from the served_entity_name which is a user provided name. For example, entity_name is the name of the Unity Catalog model.
The version of the served entity.
The version of the endpoint configuration.
The task type. Can be llm/v1/chat, llm/v1/completions, or llm/v1/embeddings.
Configurations for external models. For example, {Provider: OpenAI}
Configurations for foundation models. For example,{min_provisioned_throughput: 2200, max_provisioned_throughput: 4400}
Configurations for custom models. For example,{ min_concurrency: 0, max_concurrency: 4, compute_type: CPU }
Configurations for feature specifications. For example, { min_concurrency: 0, max_concurrency: 4, compute_type: CPU }
Timestamp of change for the served entity.
Timestamp of entity deletion. The endpoint is the container for the served entity. After the endpoint is deleted, the served entity is also deleted.

comment: Predictive optimization optimizes your data layout for peak performance and cost efficiency. This system table tracks the operation history of this feature.

columns.comment: ID of the account.
The ID of the workspace in which predictive optimization ran the operation.
The time at which the operation started. Timezone information is recorded at the end of the value with +00:00 representing UTC.
The time at which the operation ended. Timezone information is recorded at the end of the value with +00:00 representing UTC.
The name of the metastore to which the optimized table belongs.
The ID of the metastore to which the optimized table belongs.
The name of the catalog to which the optimized table belongs.
The name of the schema to which the optimized table belongs.
The name of the optimized table.
The ID of the optimized table.
The optimization operation which was performed. The value will be COMPACTION or VACUUM.
The ID for the optimization operation.
The status of the optimization operation. The value will be SUCCESSFUL or FAILED: INTERNAL_ERROR.
Additional details about the specific optimization that was performed.
The unit of usage that this operation incurred.
The amount of the usage unit that was used by this operation.
